Fair Pricing SFC-licensed virtual asset trading platforms should implement effective risk management and supervisory control measures to ensure fair pricing for the buying and selling of tokenized products on the platform. The measures should include:

a) If the proposed transaction price deviates significantly from the real-time or near-real-time indicative net asset value per unit of the relevant product (the deviation threshold will be reasonably determined taking into account the characteristics of the relevant product), an alert should be issued to investors (price deviation alert)2

b) Explaining to investors that they can choose to subscribe or redeem at net asset value (i.e., subscribe or redeem in the primary market, rather than buying and selling in the secondary market), and the related impacts thereon3; and

c) Implementing the system monitoring measures, automated pre-trade monitoring measures, and regular post-trade monitoring set out in paragraph 11.13 of the "Virtual Asset Trading Platform Guidelines," as well as other monitoring measures reasonably designed for the following purposes: preventing excessive price volatility (e.g., establishing trading volatility limits and cooling-off periods based on the last transaction price of the relevant product), preventing market manipulation, and identifying any suspected market manipulation and illegal activities.

a) Similarly, when facilitating the trading of tokenized products by their clients on the SFC-licensed virtual asset trading platform (connecting broker), 4. the SFC-licensed corporations or registered institutions shall ensure that price deviation warnings are displayed to investors on their trading interface and explain to investors the option of subscription or redemption through the primary market as described in paragraph 12(b). The SFC may require a demonstration of the trading interface, price deviation warnings and/or other relevant interfaces. Liquidity Provision Product providers shall: a) Use their best efforts to establish arrangements to ensure that each tokenized product has at least one market maker, and that at least one market maker will give not less than three months' advance notice before terminating their market maker services; b) Closely monitor the secondary market trading activities and liquidity of their tokenized products, maintain close communication with their appointed market makers, develop appropriate contingency plans, and comply with investor... (c) Appoint distributors for its tokenized products, and the distributors should be licensed corporations or registered institutions of the Securities and Futures Commission (SFC) and should be able to handle subscription and redemption requests from third-party investors, except in certain very rare cases; and (d) Establish arrangements with SFC-licensed virtual asset trading platforms to facilitate the transfer of tokenized products between the primary and secondary markets (e.g., tokens subscribed through the primary market can be easily bought and sold on the secondary market, and tokens purchased on the secondary market can also be redeemed through the primary market).

  • SFC-licensed virtual asset trading platforms 7 shall:

    a) conduct due diligence on all market makers of tokenized products approved for their platform and periodically monitor their performance against the agreed terms; and reasonably be satisfied that the market makers still possess the corresponding competence and appropriate resources to properly perform their market maker functions;

    b) ensure that all market makers approved for their platform continue to comply with established standards in terms of bid-ask spreads, quote value, minimum quote duration and participation rate;

    c) contact the market makers approved for their platform to rectify any failure to fulfill their responsibilities; and

    d) specify in their agreements with market makers: (i) the eligibility criteria and responsibilities applicable to market makers of tokenized products; and (ii) the arrangements to be implemented when a market maker ceases to provide services for a particular tokenized product.

    Distributors and market makers shall ensure compliance with applicable laws, rules, regulations and code of conduct enforced or issued by the Securities and Futures Commission (SFC) and/or other regulatory bodies, if applicable. If product providers and/or SFC-licensed virtual asset trading platforms provide remuneration and/or rewards to support market maker activities for tokenized products, such product providers and/or SFC-licensed virtual asset trading platforms shall comply with all applicable laws and regulations, including the Code of Conduct for Persons Licensed or Registered with the Securities and Futures Commission (SFC) and the relevant provisions of the Securities and Futures Ordinance, to maintain market integrity and prevent market misconduct. Disclosures The sales documents (including a product summary) for tokenized products offered for secondary market trading should clearly state: a) Risks associated with secondary market trading of tokenized products, such as liquidity risk and price deviation risk (trading may be very quiet, and the transaction price may have a significant premium/discount relative to the net asset value, especially outside of normal operating hours of the Hong Kong financial markets and on weekends), price fragmentation risk (including different transaction prices on different trading channels), and the risk of reliance on market makers; b) Key information about the trading channels (e.g., operating procedures, settlement procedures, settlement time, preset capital requirements, secondary market vs. primary market). The differences between the tokenized products and whether they can be traded across trading channels in an interoperable manner (8), the market maker arrangements (including any remuneration and/or incentives provided by the product provider and/or the SFC-licensed virtual asset trading platform to the market maker) (9), and the indicative scope of fees applicable to secondary market trading, with notes directing investors to the relevant SFC-licensed virtual asset trading platform website for details of the secondary market trading arrangements (see also paragraph 20(a));

    c) the circumstances under which secondary market trading of the tokenized products may be suspended;

    d) a list of market makers for the tokenized products (with notes directing investors to the latest list website), and any affiliated entities of the product provider acting as market makers, together with disclosures of any potential conflicts of interest.

  • SFC-licensed virtual asset trading platforms and connecting brokers shall maintain or provide access to designated online interfaces (such as websites or applications) to:

    a) Disclose details of secondary market trading arrangements for tokenized products, including trading channels, market maker arrangements (including any remuneration and/or incentives provided by the product provider and/or SFC-licensed virtual asset trading platform to market makers), market maker qualification criteria, fee schedule, and bid/ask spread 10;

    b) Issue (i) real-time or near-real-time indicative units.

    (i) Net asset value 11 (updated at least every 15 seconds during trading hours); and (ii) the latest unit net asset value 12 of the tokenized product, explaining the data source and update frequency; and (c) highlighting the relevant risks to clients intending to participate in the secondary market trading of tokenized products, such as liquidity risk and price deviation risk (trading may be very quiet, and the transaction price may have a significant premium/discount relative to the net asset value, especially outside the normal operating hours of the Hong Kong financial market and on weekends), price fragmentation risk (including different transaction prices on different trading channels), and the risk of reliance on market makers. Before opening an account for a client intending to participate in the secondary market trading of tokenized products, the SFC-licensed virtual asset trading platform and connecting broker should obtain confirmation from the client that they understand the above risks. Generally, product providers should notify the SFC in advance of any unusual circumstances related to the tokenized products they manage, including but not limited to any matters that may adversely affect the operation, secondary market trading and liquidity of their tokenized products (including receiving notification of the resignation of the last market maker). If any of the following occurs, the product provider shall notify the SFC and investors as soon as practicable: (i) trading of the tokenized product in the primary or secondary market is terminated or suspended; or (ii) market manipulation activities are terminated, interrupted, or suspended. The product provider shall include in its notification an assessment of the impact of the event on the tokenized products it manages, remedial actions, and appropriate contingency plans. C. Prior Consultation, Application, and Approval For product providers, prior consultation with the SFC is required for new investment products that intend to incorporate tokenization-related functions (primary market trading and/or secondary market trading) and require SFC approval. For existing SFC-approved investment products that intend to introduce tokenization-related functions (primary market trading and/or secondary market trading), prior consultation with the SFC and obtaining its approval are required. The SFC will assess each application on a case-by-case basis. Given the evolving environment of the tokenized market, the SFC may provide further guidance or impose additional requirements as appropriate. For secondary market trading arrangements previously approved by the SFC (e.g., trading mechanisms, price deviation alerts, market maker arrangements, and new trading channels), product providers should consult the SFC in advance regarding any subsequent material changes. Regarding intermediaries engaged in secondary market trading of tokenized products, intermediaries (including SFC-licensed virtual asset trading platforms and intermediaries intending to engage in over-the-counter secondary market trading of tokenized products) should notify their SFC case officer13 and discuss their proposed solutions14 with the case officer before engaging in secondary market trading for the first time. If there are subsequent material changes to the communicated arrangements, the intermediaries should also notify their SFC case officer and (if applicable) the HKMA. For clarification of any part of this circular, please contact us.

     

    Securities and Futures Commission  securities and the Futures Commission  Securities and Futures Affairs nbsp;      Financial Supervisory Committee 
    Investment Products Department                      Intermediary Department                             &nbsp ;                &nbsp Market Surveillance Department

SFC-licensed virtual asset trading platforms shall ensure that when the proposed transaction price deviates from the indicative net asset value per unit by more than a specified threshold, a price deviation warning is displayed on their investor trading interface. 3. The warning should state that such subscriptions and redemptions are subject to (if applicable): (i) normal primary market trading hours (e.g., open only Monday to Friday); (ii) the use of liquidity risk management tools; and (iii) an "unknown price" pricing mechanism, meaning that subscriptions and redemptions of fund units will be executed at the next calculated net asset value, which may be higher or lower than the then-current secondary market price. 4. A connecting broker generally refers to an entity that transmits clients' secondary market trading instructions to a SFC-licensed virtual asset trading platform. Connecting brokers shall comply with paragraph 18 and Schedule 7 of the Code of Conduct for Licensed or Registered Persons of the Securities and Futures Commission. 5. For example, contingency plans should include: (i) whether secondary market trading of tokenized products needs to be temporarily suspended when primary market trading is suspended; and (ii) arrangements to ensure that backup market makers can be arranged and activated when needed (especially in extreme market conditions). 6. Please refer to Question 1 of the "Frequently Asked Questions on Exchange Traded Funds and Listed Funds" (English version only). 7. Market maker mechanisms (including market maker access) are generally managed by the platform operator. Given that some market makers may directly contact the platform operator without contacting their product providers to participate in market maker activities for a particular product, the primary responsibility for accepting market makers and monitoring their performance rests with the platform operator. 8. For the avoidance of doubt, units of tokenized products can be transferred, for example, to support cross-trading of tokenized products in an interoperable manner. 9. The disclosures should aim to assist investors in assessing the liquidity and supply and demand of tokenized products on SFC-licensed virtual asset trading platforms. 10. In addition to the requirements set out in paragraph 20, SFC-licensed virtual asset trading platforms and connecting brokers should also comply with other existing disclosure requirements. 11. Indicative net asset value (NAV) refers to an indicative real-time estimate of the NAV per unit of a tokenized product, calculated during the trading hours of the product on the SFC-licensed virtual asset trading platform, and generally based on the most recently available market prices of the product's portfolio components. 12. Latest NAV refers to the latest official NAV per unit of a tokenized product, calculated based on the relevant valuation date of its constituent documents on the most recent primary market trading day. 13. In accordance with the Circular on Intermediaries Engaging in Activities Related to Tokenized Securities, the registrar should also notify the Hong Kong Monetary Authority.

    14. The relevant notification should be made as soon as practicable. For example, when a product provider consults the SFC in advance pursuant to paragraphs 23 to 26, the relevant SFC-licensed virtual asset trading platform, SFC-licensed corporation, and registration authority should simultaneously notify the SFC and (if applicable) the HKMA.

data.title: Stocks and startups affected by the release of Claude Design
data.description:
On Sunday morning, at table number six of Zombie Café on California Ave, my third espresso finally arrived. Anthropic's new tool, Claude Design, launched last Friday, is something Silicon Valley's Alan Walker has reviewed twice. Let me first break down this tool for you—what it is, why it's not just "just another AI design tool," and why it's being priced as a structural event in the US and Hong Kong stock markets. Once I've explained the tool clearly, I'll name the companies it has already cut and those it will cut, one by one. What is Claude Design? · A Sketch of a New Knife To put it simply—you open Claude, type a sentence in the dialog box: "I want a SaaS pricing page, dark theme, three tiers," and it immediately gives you an interactive prototype. You continue by saying, "Mark the second tier as recommended," and it changes it. You say, "Change the color scheme to our company's brand colors," and it reads your codebase and Figma files and automatically aligns them. After completion, package it with one click for Claude Code; the code will be automatically generated and ready for deployment. It sounds like a combination of Figma + Gamma + v0? Yes, that's exactly what it means. But the combination is just the surface; the real destructive power lies in the following three details. Why is it a killer weapon? • Three underlying details • The first strike: Directly consuming the codebase and Figma files. During the onboarding phase, Claude Design directly reads your company's GitHub codebase and Figma design files, automatically generating a dynamic design system—colors, typography, components, tokens, all aligned perfectly. Afterward, all your projects automatically apply this system. This cut eliminates the very reason for the existence of the entire design system tooling category over the past decade. Companies like Supernova, ZeroHeight, Specify, Knapsack, Tokens Studio, and Frontify essentially help maintain consistency between Figma and code. When Claude reads directly from the source, the intermediate synchronization layer is unnecessary. This category is completely reset. The second step: Handoff Bundle to Claude Code. Once the design is complete, package it into a handoff bundle with one click, then submit it to Claude Code; the code will be automatically generated. There's a severely underrated line in the press release: "without code review or PRs." This statement is a death sentence for the design-to-code industry chain of the past decade.

  • Zeplin Make design-to-dev middle layer

  • Anima, Locofy Convert Figma to React

  • v0Convert prompt to React component

  • The Third Cut · Frontier Design · Non-Designers Can Also Create Shaders, 3D, Voice, and Video

    The most ruthless line in the press release——

    Anyone

    This rhythm becomes clear after watching it twice: Krieger can't stay on Figma's board for even a day longer, because the time to fire has already been set. This isn't called "intensifying competition," it's called "premeditated." Having been in Silicon Valley for twenty years, the real battles never begin with the product launch, but with the disclosure of board seats a week before. Figma: From $60 Billion to $10 Billion in Just One Year. When Figma went public last year, Dylan Field famously said on stage, "Figma is where teams come together." At that time, the company was valued at $60 billion, and its IPO day saw a significant increase. Less than a year later, its market capitalization plummeted to $10 billion by Friday's closing, a drop of over 80% from its peak. This isn't just a fluctuation; it's a rewriting of a company's valuation paradigm. Figma fell 7.28% on Friday, which was actually milder than expected—because the real blow came the moment Krieger resigned last Tuesday, when it already fell 6% that day. Combined, Figma's market capitalization evaporated by more than a billion dollars this week alone. Some might ask, doesn't Figma have a moat? Multi-user collaboration, plugin ecosystem, designer identity—things Claude couldn't create overnight. That's true. So Figma won't fall 20% in a day like LegalZoom; it will slowly de-rate—within two years, its forward PE will drop from 60 to 20, and its market capitalization will be halved again. This is an old story in SaaS—when a company's growth expectations change from "sustained high growth" to "stable cash flow," the valuation multiple will be compressed to one-third of its original value. A friend of mine at Benchmark called me last Wednesday, saying that internally they've lowered Figma's target multiple from 12x sales to 4x sales. This isn't just his opinion; it's a consensus across the growth-equity sector. Those crossover funds from Figma's IPO will have a tough year. Figma has only one way to survive—not by relying on "growth," but by relying on its "institutional moat." Millions of designers worldwide store their work, teams, and historical versions on Figma; this represents migration costs. It's like Slack being besieged by Microsoft Teams but surviving. The Figma that survives isn't the Figma we know; it's a mature software company that pays dividends per share. That's what its valuation will be. Adobe: An 18-Year Veteran's Choice to Hand Over the Blade at This Time Adobe's story is more complex than Figma's. I met Shantanu Narayen at the Adobe Summit in 2015. He served as CEO for 18 years, leading Adobe from a $30 billion to a $200 billion company. On March 12th, he announced his handover, and the stock price fell 7.6% that day, and has since fallen 25% year-to-date, nearing a three-year low. My perspective on Adobe differs from many others. Many believe Narayen was ousted by AI—no. He clearly understood what Adobe had to do next, something he couldn't do himself. Adobe's next step is a "native AI overhaul"—that is, to completely redesign Creative Cloud, turning it into an agent-first product line. Narayen can't do this because he's the hero of the subscription transformation victory; his entire mental model is subscription. To burn the old subscription model, they need an unburdened "AI-native CEO." The market is waiting for someone. Internally, David Wadhwani is the most popular choice, while externally, they are reportedly vetting several executives from the SaaS and AI sectors. My assessment is—

    But regardless of the situation, Adobe's long-term story is no longer about "subscription dominance."

    After the Claude Design announcement on Friday, Adobe only fell 1.5%, which seems respectable, but this is only because the news of Narayen's resignation has already been priced in. The real pain hasn't arrived yet; we'll have to wait for the guidance of the Q2 financial report.

    Canva, Wix, and a whole list of less conspicuous failures. Canva CEO Melanie Perkins gave an endorsement in a Claude Design press release, saying, "bringing Canva to wherever ideas begin." Silicon Valley insiders understand the meaning behind this—it's the CEO of a $42B company publicly admitting that the top of the funnel is no longer with her. Canva's internal shareholder valuation last year was $60B, dropping to $42B this year. If it does IPO next year, the expected pricing is around $30B, unless Melanie can come up with a sufficiently strong agentic strategy. I've been following Wix. It fell 4.7% on Tuesday, and its year-to-date performance isn't great, but with a current share price of only $20, a forward PE of 13, and free cash flow of $570 million, it's cheap. Motley Fool has already started calling for "buy the dip." My assessment is different—Wix is ​​a typical example of a "deep value" and a "value trap" hybrid. Its DIY website building business is perfectly positioned to exploit the weaknesses of the Claude Design + Claude Code combination. SMB customers are already a high-churn group; when they discover they can create a website twice as good-looking as a Wix template using Claude Chat, migration won't be a problem. GoDaddy fell 3% on the same day. The logic is exactly the same as Wix, except GoDaddy's users are older and more engaged. It won't fall much in the short term, but it will definitely de-rate in the long term. There's also a whole row of companies you might not have considered: This isn't a single event; it's an entire industry chain. The worst performer was Thomson Reuters—its stock fell 15.83% on February 3rd, the largest single-day drop in the company's history, due to the launch of a legal plugin by Claude Cowork. LegalZoom fell 19.68% that day. The three traditional Achilles' heel of SaaS—legal, accounting, and data services—have been breached in the past three months. Gartner also fell 21% on the same day; the business of "manually curating information" like research reports is no longer worth as much in the face of LLM (Legal, Accounting, and Data) services.

    Three Waves of Bullets · SaaSpocalypse Timeline

    Pull up the timeline and you'll understand what Anthropic has been doing this year.

    Four months, three waves of bullets. Each wave hit a different category, but behind it is the same logic—LLM native products replacing SaaS middleware.

    Anthropic's valuation has risen from $380 billion to reportedly rejecting an $800 billion investment offer. If the Anthropic founding team believes its valuation will break the trillion-dollar mark next year, it means they still have a lot of unreleased assets in their arsenal. Several of my hedge fund friends are currently focused on one thing—where will the next shot be fired? There's a chart from Goldman Sachs Prime Brokerage that I keep looking at. The difference between hedge fund holdings in semiconductors and software has reached a record high. Semiconductors are overflowing with net long positions, while software is overflowing with net short positions. This isn't a short-term trend; it's a consensus-driven, heavily invested sector across the industry. You don't need smart money; you just need to see where the money is, and the story unfolds there.

    Those AI Startups Directly Hit by Claude

    This section is discussed separately. The hottest sector in Silicon Valley over the past two years has been "AI design tools" or "vibe coding." Several players have raised substantial funds—Gamma, which makes AI pitch decks, had a valuation of $600 million last year and boasts an impressive user base. Its focus is 100% overlapping with Claude Design's pitch deck functionality. Today, Gamma's board must be holding a conference call discussing "How can we become a feature?" The answer is difficult because Gamma uses the Claude API—the underlying model is someone else's, and now that someone else is directly involved, the branding and distribution are in their hands. v0 by Vercel has always been the most elegant product in the design-to-code space. Today, v0's core proposition has been directly devoured by Claude Design + Claude Code. Vercel, the parent company, is protected by Next.js and deployment infrastructure, but v0, as an independent product line, needs to redefine its position. The most likely path is for v0 to be downgraded to a deployment target for Claude Design, transforming from a peer competitor into a downstream execution layer. This path isn't shameful, but it's certainly not attractive. Lovable is a company I've always liked. It just completed its $330 million Series B funding round last December, valuing the company at $6.6 billion. Its investor list is impressive—CapitalG, NVIDIA Ventures, Salesforce Ventures, Databricks Ventures, Atlassian Ventures, and HubSpot Ventures. ARR surged from $100M in July to $400M in February, a very aggressive jump. However, Lovable's core technology strategy is "using Claude to build apps"—their own model is Claude. Now that Claude is handling app generation themselves, the legitimacy of this proposition is being shaken. Lovable may survive for a while longer with full-stack deployment and Supabase integration, but the pressure to re-rate its valuation is already there. Bolt.new (backed by StackBlitz), in the same sector, has 90% overlap in their propositions. Their WebContainer technology is differentiated, but whether it's enough against platform-level competitors is hard to say. Galileo AI, Uizard, and Visily are smaller players with highly homogeneous propositions, lacking distribution and moat. The investors in these companies were preparing for discussions this weekend regarding the next round of valuation reductions or early exits. A friend of mine who does early-stage investing sent me a screenshot last week of urgent emails from the CEOs of three AI design tool companies in his portfolio. All three emails used the same word – "pivot." It's unclear where the pivot lies, but the word "pivot" is there. This is the real scene in Silicon Valley. The Hong Kong Stock Market Massacre of February 4th: Now that the story of the US stock market is finished, I want to dedicate more space to the Hong Kong and A-share markets – because these are less discussed by mainstream financial media, but there are more misjudgments and opportunities there than in the US stock market. Rewind. On February 4th, US-listed SaaS stocks were battered overnight by Claude Cowork's legal plugin scandal. The sentiment spread to Asia the following morning, resulting in a near-total collapse of the Hong Kong-listed SaaS sector: The total market capitalization of the Hong Kong-listed SaaS sector evaporated by nearly HK$15 billion that day. The sentiment continued to spread: Tencent fell 3.96%, Alibaba fell 0.93%, NetEase fell 3.32%, Baidu fell 2.97%, and the Hang Seng Tech Index dropped 1.84%. The A-share market fared even worse – Sangfor Technologies fell 4.86%, Yonyou Network fell 3.27%, Hand Enterprise Solutions fell 7.41%, and Gravity Media and Tiandi Online were locked at their daily limit down. My first reaction to these numbers was – an overreaction. It's not that Hong Kong-listed SaaS companies are without problems; of course, there are. But the underlying logic of these Hong Kong-listed companies is different from that of US-listed companies. The US SaaS market is a highly mature, established market. Companies like Salesforce, Workday, and Atlassian have already completed their digitization processes, and AI is consuming their subscription revenue. Hong Kong-listed and A-share SaaS companies serve Chinese enterprises still in the early stages of digitization – clients of companies like Kingdee and Yonyou, many still migrating from Excel to ERP. AI is an accelerator for them, not a replacement. That's why I added some positions in Hong Kong-listed SaaS companies on February 5th – not all, but selectively. The mispricing created by sentiment-driven selling presents an alpha opportunity.

    Hong Kong Stocks: What I Will Buy and What I Will Stay Away From

    What I Will Buy

    Kingsoft Cloud (03896.HK)—A structural winner. Its share price has risen nearly 6.5 times in the past six months, and its market capitalization now exceeds HK$32 billion. Its logic is clean—the demand for computing power is exploding in the AI ​​era, and China's computing infrastructure is in a window of opportunity for localization. Kingsoft Cloud is the infrastructure provider for Xiaomi's AI strategy and WPS's AI strategy. At the Xiaomi press conference on February 27th, Lei Jun publicly stated that the R&D budget for 2025 would exceed 30 billion yuan, with AI accounting for a quarter of that, and Kingsoft Cloud being the most direct beneficiary. This isn't sentiment play; it's revenue visibility play. Zhipu Huazhang (02513.HK) — The "world's first large-scale model stock" listed on January 8th, it closed up 13.17% on its first day of trading, and its market capitalization has remained around 90 billion Hong Kong dollars for the past two weeks. On February 21st, after the Spring Festival, it opened with a single-day increase of 42.72%, and its market capitalization once exceeded 323.2 billion Hong Kong dollars. This isn't supported by performance figures, but by the narrative of "domestic substitution." Given that Anthropic's Claude is not compliant with regulations in China, companies need domestically produced LLMs, and Zhipu is one of the top choices. This is policy beta, not performance alpha, but this beta will continue for the next two years. MiniMax — Listed on January 9th, it opened 78% higher and closed up 109.09%, pushing its market capitalization past HK$100 billion, recently reaching HK$304.2 billion. The M2.5 model, launched in February, is touted as a competitor to Claude Opus 4.6. Its three C-end product lines—Conch AI, Talkie, and Hoshino—have achieved a 71% revenue share from C-end customers in overseas markets with strong payment capabilities. I'll keep an eye on this company but won't invest heavily—C-end AI companion products carry copyright risks, and overseas regulatory windows could tighten at any time. Meitu Inc. (01357.HK) — The most perplexing company. On one hand, Claude Design's image generation capabilities are indeed an upstream threat to Meitu's AI design tools. On the other hand, Meitu's accumulated 12.61 million paid subscribers in the Chinese market (a 38.4% year-on-year increase), along with its localized beauty, social, and e-commerce scenarios, form a moat that Anthropic cannot penetrate in the short term. In the past two years, Meitu's market capitalization has risen from $3 billion to HK$29 billion, an increase of 1300%—already pricing in many optimistic expectations. Neutral to slightly bullish, no additional holdings, no liquidation, let's wait for the Q2 financial report before making further decisions. Weimob Group (02013.HK) — Small but beautiful. Just released its 2025 annual report, achieving its first annual profit since 2021 of RMB 42.4 million, and disclosing for the first time that AI-related annual revenue exceeded RMB 116 million. The HK$1.8 share price corresponds to the bottom of the SaaS winter. If AI revenue continues to grow in the following quarters, this is a small-cap stock with a potential of 2-3 times. However, there are also risks – Weimob's basic SaaS subscription business is also facing the impact of AI, and it is uncertain whether AI revenue can outpace the decline in subscription business. Kingdee International (00268.HK) – Although it may be oversold after falling more than 12% in February, structurally its cloud ERP business is the most direct replacement target for AI agents. The core value of ERP is "workflow accumulation + data accumulation," and Claude Cowork, plus the enterprise data connector, is swallowing up the "workflow accumulation" part. If Kingdee doesn't take drastic steps towards rapid AI adoption, its valuation multiple will continue to decline. Kingsoft (03888.HK) — WPS Office is the target of Claude Design and Cowork. The Chinese market has a domestic product protection period, but I estimate this period will only last 12-18 months. After that, local players (ByteDance, Alibaba) will focus on the Chinese office market, rapidly diluting Kingsoft's competitive advantage. China Software International (00354.HK) and Mobvista (01860.HK) — The former is an IT outsourcing company, with AI directly replacing code outsourcing; the latter is a mobile advertising SaaS company, and Claude Cowork's marketing plugin will gradually consume long-tail advertising workflows. Both companies face long-term structural headwinds; avoid participating in short-term rebounds.

    China's AI Startups: A Completely Different Track

    At this point, we must discuss the Chinese AI startups separately, because their situation regarding Claude Design is completely different from that of Silicon Valley startups.

    To put it simply—Claude Design's direct impact on China's six AI giants is almost zero. The reason is simple: Anthropic is unavailable in China. The six companies—Zhipu, MiniMax, Moon's Dark Side, Baichuan, Jieyue, and Lingyi—along with slightly later additions like Mianbi and DeepSeek, are not competing with Claude, but with each other, ByteDance, Alibaba, and Tencent's hybrid ecosystem.

    Zhipu, MiniMax, Moon's Dark Side, Baichuan, Jieyue, and Lingyi, along with slightly later additions like Mianbi and DeepSeek, are not competing with Claude, but with each other, ByteDance, Alibaba, and Tencent's hybrid ecosystem.

    Zhipu, MiniMax, Moon's Dark Side, Baichuan, Jieyue, and Lingyi, and slightly later additions like Mianbi and DeepSeek, are not competing with Claude, but with each other, ByteDance, Alibaba, and Tencent's hybrid ecosystem.

    Zhipu, MiniMax, Moon's Dark Side, Baichuan, Jieyue, and Lingyi, as well as slightly later additions like Mianbi and DeepSeek.

    Zhipu, MiniMax, Moon's Dark Side, Baichuan, Jieyue, and Lingyi, are competing with each other, ByteDance, Alibaba, and Tencent's hybrid ecosystem.

    Zhipu, MiniMax, Moon's Dark Side, Baichuan, Jieyue, Lingyi, and DeepSeek.

    Zhi But this doesn't mean they aren't under pressure. The pressure comes from another direction—the benchmark for model valuation in the capital market is being redefined by Claude's valuation curve. The opening act of China's AI in 2026 is quite exciting: This is the 2026 China AI unicorn differentiation chart—only three top players remain in the foundation model: Zhipu, MiniMax, and Lunar Dark Side, plus three major companies: ByteDance, Alibaba, and DeepSeek, forming a basically solidified six-way competitive landscape. The core problem facing these three independent model companies isn't Claude Design—it's their own rate of cash burn: They need to continuously raise funds, and their benchmark is Anthropic—Anthropic had no pressure to be profitable until it reached a $10 billion ARR because Google and Amazon poured billions of dollars into it. Can Chinese model companies raise similar amounts of money? Currently, it seems the only options are relying on the "national team" (government-backed investment) and a Hong Kong IPO. Zhipu has chosen a Hong Kong IPO, MiniMax is following suit, and Moon's Dark Side is also planning an IPO in the second half of this year. This is a race where "fundraising equals survival." The impact of Claude Design's product launches on them isn't direct competition, but rather prompting global investors to reassess the value of integrated "model + product" models—Anthropic's self-developed product lowers the valuation ceiling for "model-only companies." Two weeks ago, I had dinner with a product director from Kimi. His exact words were, "The Claude Design release was an alarm clock for us, reminding us not to just make models, but to build end-to-end products and an ecosystem." This is the next step for Chinese model companies—from selling APIs to selling scenarios. Alibaba Cloud's move to bundle four models—Qianwen 3.5, GLM-5, Kimi K2.5, and MiniMax M2.5—for 7.9 yuan/month is laying the distribution channel for this. There's another line that's easily overlooked—**application-layer AI companies**. Chinese AI application companies, such as *Natural Selection* (AI companion), *Future Pair* (emotional robots), and *MarsWave* (generative audio), are actually more likely to survive than model companies because they solve specific scenario problems, not an endless arms race of computing power. Claude Design is an opportunity, not a threat, for them—if Claude Design's design capabilities can be packaged into APIs, the development costs for these companies will drop significantly. The Next Shot: What I'm Waiting For Here. Claude Design is not the end; it's the third bullet (Cowork, Code, Design) for Anthropic Labs, this product factory. Where will the next shot land? In two directions. **Direction 1: Marketing and Growth Full Stack** Claude Cowork already has a marketing plugin. If Anthropic launches a product like "Claude Growth Studio," HubSpot (HUBS) will likely experience a Figma-level single-day plunge, with Klaviyo, Braze, and Marketo all following suit. I think this direction has a high probability of success because marketing automation is the easiest product for Anthropic Labs to quantify ROI for. If I were to hedge, I would buy put spreads before HubSpot's earnings report. **Direction Two: Analytics and BI** Tableau (owned by Salesforce), Looker (owned by Google), and ThoughtSpot. Claude Cowork already performs data analysis, so native dashboard generation and interactive data exploration are the next logical steps. Snowflake and Databricks, these data layers, will benefit in this direction, but application-level BI tools will be impacted. Salesforce, as the parent company of Tableau, will continue to face pressure.

    Direction Three: Further Erosion of Enterprise Development Toolchains

    Atlassian (TEAM)'s Jira and Confluence, and ServiceNow's ITSM, all have the potential for restructuring. ServiceNow, because it locks up a large amount of enterprise process data, may actually survive longer; Atlassian, lacking a deep "system of record" moat, is more vulnerable.

    The Zombie staff started clearing out the cups from the morning rush hour; the Stanford guy at the next table had already left. Finally, let me tell you what I accomplished this weekend.

    ... My portfolio operations. [Image of a file: https://img.jinse.com.cn/7454119_image3.png](https://img.jinse.com.cn/7454119_image3.png) [Image of a file: 8EHEZicUsbYZnd0X2EfFhscf5GWTVSOohEasSFki.png](https://img.jinse.com.cn/7454119_image3.png)](https://img.jinse.com.cn/7454119_image3.png) [Image of a file: 8EHEZicUsbYZnd0X2EfFhscf5GWTVSOohEasSFki.png](https://EHEZicUsbYZnd0X2EfFhscf5GWTVSOohEasSFki.png)](https://EHEZicUsbYZnd0X2EfFhscf5GWTVSOohEasSFki.png)](https://img.jinse.com.cn/7454119_image3 ... Where will Anthropic's next shot land? I don't know. But I do know one thing—**three bullets in four months, the next one will most likely be at the end of May or the beginning of June.** That day, the market will tremble again, and we'll be sitting here again, watching another string of names.

data.title: From KelpDAO to Aave Bad Debts: How Cross-Chain Vulnerabilities Breach the DeFi Ecosystem
data.description:
Tao Zhu, Jinse Finance

Summary

On April 19, 2026, KelpDAO suffered a loss of approximately $292 million due to a cross-chain bridge vulnerability. The core of the attack stemmed from a single point of trust failure in LayerZero Labs' cross-chain verification mechanism, allowing attackers to forge cross-chain messages and "mint coins out of thin air." The incident quickly spread throughout the DeFi ecosystem, with multiple protocols suspending cross-chain functionality.

KelpDAO suffered a loss of nearly $300 million, setting a new record for the largest DeFi security incident this year. The essence of this incident is not a traditional smart contract vulnerability, but a systemic risk event caused by "cross-chain trust model failure + amplification of DeFi composability," highlighting the structural problems in cross-chain security and collateral asset risk management.

This article reviews the KelpDAO theft, analyzes the causes and perpetrators, summarizes the losses, and discusses why Aave generates a large number of bad debts.

I. The KelpDAO Attack Summary

At 4:10 AM on April 19, 2026, KelpDAO posted an X post:

Earlier today, we discovered suspicious cross-chain activity involving rsETH. During the investigation, we have suspended rsETH contracts on the mainnet and multiple L2 layers.

We are working with @LayerZero_Core, @unichain, our auditors, and top security experts on a root cause analysis (RCA).

We will keep you updated on the latest developments.

Please follow the official @KelpDAO account for the latest news.

According to blockchain security company Cyvers, attackers exploited the rsETH adapter bridging contract (software code that manages Kelp's rsETH tokens) to steal approximately $293 million from the platform.

A $293 million vulnerability was detected: Cyvers AI system detected a massive attack against @KelpDAO

Our platform flagged the attack in real time and tracked approximately $293.7 million stolen from the protocol's RSETH adapter.

Our platform flagged the attack in real time and tracked approximately $293.7 million stolen from the protocol's RSETH adapter.

Currently, approximately $250 million has been converted into ETH and distributed across two chains: Attacker address: 0x8B1b6c9A6DB1304000412dd21Ae6A70a82d60D3b • Ethereum: $178 million • Arbitrum: $72 million Analysis Insight: Our analysis shows that the attacker is linked to the source of funds in Tornado Cash. We are tracing complex layered operations conducted through intermediary wallets (0xbb6... and 0x1f4...) designed to obfuscate the flow of funds. Cyvers is actively monitoring the situation.

II. Why was it stolen?

The KelpDAO attack was a typical systemic security incident caused by a failure of the cross-chain trust model coupled with configuration errors. Its core causes can be summarized in three aspects: design flaws in the cross-chain verification mechanism, the potential for compromise of key private keys/verification nodes, and the amplification of risks associated with DeFi composition.

1. Root Cause: LayerZero Cross-Chain Verification's "Single Point of Trust" Failure SlowMist's Yu Xian published a preliminary analysis of the Kelp security incident involving the theft of 116,500 rsETH on the X platform. He pointed out: Kelp used a 1/1 DVN configuration for its LayerZero cross-chain service, the classic "single signature" configuration, while the official LayerZero documentation recommends a 2/2 DVN configuration by default. This "single signature, single point of trust" may have been compromised by social engineering techniques (this is speculation, pending further investigation). The core issue in this attack lies in the design of the cross-chain bridge. KelpDAO's rsETH uses LayerZero Labs' OFT (Omnichain Fungible Token) bridging solution, and the security of this bridge relies on DVN (Decentralized Validation Network). Because KelpDAO uses a single validator node, only one validator node signature is needed to confirm the "authenticity" of a cross-chain message. That is, an attacker only needs to forge one verification to release real assets. In practice, the attacker constructed a forged lzReceive call, deceiving the bridge contract into releasing 116,500 rsETH (approximately $292 million). 2. Technical Trigger: Validator Node/Private Key May Be Compromised Regarding how the attack achieves "forged verification," current mainstream analysis focuses on two possibilities: DVN Validator Node Compromised: That is, the attacker may interfere with normal nodes through network attacks (such as DDoS), forcing the system to rely on a controlled validator node.

Private Key Leakage: This means the private key of the DVN or source chain OApp node is leaked, allowing attackers to directly sign legitimate cross-chain messages.

3. DeFi Composition Amplifies Risk

Hackers forged cross-chain messages, called the LayerZero Endpoint, and submitted forged verifications; subsequently, the bridge contract mistakenly believed that the cross-chain assets were locked, when in fact there was no collateral; after completing the attack, the hacker did not directly sell rsETH, but instead used it as collateral in the lending protocol to borrow ETH, which, while avoiding the risk of a market crash, transferred the risk from a single protocol to the entire DeFi system.

In summary, the essence of the KelpDAO theft is not a code vulnerability, but a "systemic collapse of the cross-chain trust model under a single-point configuration," which was amplified into an industry-wide risk event through the composability of DeFi.

III. Who orchestrated this attack? LayerZero believes that initial indications suggest the attack may have been carried out by a highly sophisticated state actor, most likely the Lazarus Group of the Democratic People's Republic of Korea, more specifically TraderTraitor. IV. Who is suffering losses? 1. Kelp DAO The most direct loser is undoubtedly Kelp DAO—it suffered approximately $292 million in losses. This is the largest hacking incident this year so far. 2. Aave Aave was hit hardest, with attackers using uncollateralized rsETH as collateral to borrow ETH. The potential bad debt on the mainnet is approximately 524,000 ETH ($123.4 million), with an additional 307,000 ETH ($72.4 million) in bad debt. As a result, the price of AAVE fell to a low of around $90, a 24.15% drop from the 7-day high of $118.65, and was trading at $91.45 at the time of writing. Due to a large number of bad debts on Aave, numerous whales subsequently withdrew ETH from Aave, pushing the ETH utilization rate on Aave to 100%. According to on-chain analyst Ember, over $5.4 billion in assets were urgently withdrawn from Aave after hackers borrowed a large amount of ETH by staking illegally minted rsETH on Aave. This includes Justin Sun retrieving 65,584 ETH ($154 million) from Aave, and others including: Abraxas Capital withdrawing $392 million; MEXC withdrawing $431 million; and whale 0x7CD0 (possibly linked to Nonco) withdrawing $405.7 million. Aave officially responded in a post: The rsETH market on Aave V3 and Aave V4 has been frozen. Aave's contracts were not attacked; the attack is related to rsETH itself. This freeze is due to an attack on the Kelp DAO rsETH bridge. The rsETH market was frozen to prevent new rsETH deposits and collateralized loans while the situation is assessed. We are reviewing rsETH lending information that occurred on Aave after the attack and will share more details as soon as possible. If this incident results in bad debts in the protocol, we will explore ways to mitigate the losses. On April 20th, Aave released another update on the rsETH incident: Analysis shows that rsETH on the Ethereum mainnet is fully collateralized. As a precaution, rsETH remains frozen on Aave V3 and V4. WETH reserves have also been frozen in affected markets, including Ethereum, Arbitrum, Base, Mantle, and Linea. Aave is actively verifying information and evaluating potential solutions. Curve founder Michael Egorov stated: "Hopefully Aave can resolve this issue. The Kelp DAO attack, which resulted in approximately $292 million in losses and bad debts for Aave, highlights the risks inherent in the currently prevalent 'non-isolated lending' model. This model offers good scalability but carries higher risk, making risk management crucial (Aave has a strong track record in this area). One approach is a fully isolated model like Curve Finance's markets, while another is a hybrid model (complex but feasible). However, the market hasn't fully grasped the advantages of these solutions. Aave v4's hub and spoke model may be a step towards a semi-isolated, more secure direction." They are also potential bad debt absorbers. Following the incident, Euler issued a statement: "Statement Regarding the KelpDAO rsETH Incident" Euler's core protocols and smart contracts are secure, reliable, and functioning normally. The identified risk exposure is limited and has been proactively assessed. Approximately $1.7 million in risk exposure has been identified across a range of Euler Prime trading pairs on the Ethereum mainnet: → rsETH / wstETH → rsETH / wETH → rsETH / wBTC These risk exposures have been contained within a certain range, representing only a small portion of the overall trading activity. We are actively monitoring the situation and closely watching for potential secondary impacts while investigating the entire incident. 4. Compound After obtaining the aberrantly minted rsETH, the attackers deposited it into Compound for collateralized lending. The scale of bad debts on Compound is not yet clearly defined, but it is included in the affected protocol category. Security researchers recommend that Compound lower its collateral factor and limit the rsETH market. As of press time, there has been no official statement. 5. LayerZero and Other Projects The hackers launched the attack based on the LayerZero OFT cross-chain bridge, which is the entry point for the vulnerability. On April 19th, LayerZero posted: "We have a full understanding of the rsETH vulnerability incident and have been actively working with the KelpDAO team to fix it since the incident occurred, while continuously monitoring the situation. Other applications remain secure. We are working with teams such as the Security Alliance to identify the root cause. Once we have all the information, we will release a full post-mortem report with KelpDAO." Due to security risks, several protocols have suspended related functions or marketplaces to prevent potential risks. Hyperwave, a DeFi project within the Hyperliquid ecosystem, issued an announcement: We are actively monitoring the Kelp rsETH incident and working closely with security partners to ensure Hyperwave assets are protected from any potential impact. As a precautionary measure, all LayerZero bridging for Hyperwave assets has been temporarily suspended. etherFi stated: Liquid Vault is not currently directly exposed to the risks of the Kelp rsETH incident, but as a precaution, the LayerZero cross-chain bridging function between weETH and eETH has been suspended until the root cause of the Kelp rsETH incident is clarified. Meanwhile, for Liquid (ETH, BTC, USD), sETHFI, and eBTC products, the relevant Teller contracts have been suspended to block the LayerZero OFT bridging path. Deposit and withdrawal functions for these assets have also been suspended. We are currently working closely with security partners and will update the progress as more details are confirmed. In addition, protocols such as Ethena, Lombard, Euler Labs, TRON DAO, ApeCoin/ApeChain, mETH Protocol, Solv Protocol, MOCA Foundation, and River have all suspended the LayerZero OFT cross-chain bridge. Morpho stated: Shortly after the KelpDAO and LayerZero incidents, Morpho, as a precautionary measure, suspended the MORPHO token's OFT cross-chain bridge on Arbitrum. Arbitrum's cross-chain functionality will remain suspended until the root cause of the rsETH incident is determined. Polygon posted on the X platform: The team has been actively monitoring the rsETH vulnerability: the Polygon chain, Agglayer, and the broader ecosystem including Katana and Vaultbridge have not been affected. To date, Polygon has safely transferred over $2 trillion, and we will continue to be vigilant and monitor developments. V. Why did Aave incur bad debts? In this KelpDAO attack, Aave was the biggest "victim," not because its smart contracts were compromised, but because of the structural limitations of its lending model when faced with "distorted collateral." First, let's look at Aave's basic operating logic: Aave is essentially an overcollateralized lending protocol. Its core rule is: users must first deposit collateral assets (such as ETH, stETH, rsETH, etc.); borrow other assets at a certain collateral-to-value (LTV) ratio; if the collateral value falls below the liquidation threshold, liquidation will be triggered. Aave does not verify whether assets are created out of thin air, whether there is underlying collateral, or whether they originate from abnormal cross-chain behavior. Hackers exploited a cross-chain vulnerability to "forge" a large amount of rsETH, which are completely legitimate ERC-20 assets on-chain. From Aave's perspective, these rsETH are normal assets. The hackers first deposited the forged rsETH into Aave, and then borrowed real assets such as ETH and stablecoins. Under normal circumstances, if users default, the liquidator will buy back the collateral assets, and the protocol will recover the losses. However, in this attack, rsETH was essentially "generated without collateral," with no one willing to take over, rendering the liquidation mechanism ineffective. Ultimately, this leads to the inability to recover borrowed assets and realize collateralized assets. The Aave contract itself doesn't have vulnerabilities; the problem lies in Aave trusting the forged assets, thus facing trust risks. Appendix: Introduction to KelpDAO and rsETH KelpDAO is a decentralized protocol focused on "liquidity restaking." Its core goal is to release asset liquidity and improve yield efficiency within the Ethereum restaking ecosystem. Initiated by members of the Stader Labs team, the project is primarily built around the EigenLayer ecosystem. KelpDAO's core innovation lies in converting "locked assets" into "circulating assets." Users can deposit ETH or other LSDs (such as stETH, ETHx) into the protocol, participate in restaking, and receive corresponding liquidity tokens (rsETH). In this way, users can continue to earn staking and restaking rewards while also using the asset for lending, trading, or other DeFi scenarios, thus achieving "yield synergy." rsETH is the core asset issued by KelpDAO and is a typical liquidity restaking token. It represents the "equity certificate" of ETH restaking by users in KelpDAO and its future yield. Each rsETH theoretically corresponds to one restaking asset and its yield right. This design is similar to stETH in liquidity staking, but further adds "restaking yield." Holders not only receive ETH staking yield but also restaking and other protocol rewards, achieving multiple sources of income. Because rsETH simultaneously possesses "asset attributes + collateral attributes + liquidity attributes," it is widely accepted in the DeFi system. However, precisely because of this high composability, once its underlying mechanism encounters a problem (such as the recent cross-chain vulnerability), the risk can be rapidly amplified and transmitted to the entire system.

data.title: The real AI token is USDC.
data.description:
Author: Vaidik Mandloi Source: tokendispatch Translation: Shan Ouba, Jinse Finance

Somewhere on the internet right now, a program is independently operating an entire company.

It's called Felix, and the company is called OpenClaw. Felix sells a $29 AI money-making guide PDF. Ironically, Felix itself makes the money, while this PDF teaches you how to make money using it. It runs an online store called Clawmart, proactively reaching potential customers through a voice API. When it encounters a task it can't handle, it goes online, hires other AI agents, pays them, and then continues operating.

According to my previous calculations, Felix has generated approximately $195,000 in revenue, with monthly operating costs of around $1,500, almost entirely spent on large model calls. Legally, this company is a Class C corporation, owned by Nat Eliason, who is almost entirely uninvolved in its operations. He makes no day-to-day decisions; he simply owns this AI agent. Consider this: this is software with its own wallet, a fully automated, self-growing real-world enterprise. Each month, it pays for its servers and models, sustaining itself with virtually no human intervention.

Source: Felixcraft

Felix is ​​just a small example. There's an even more exaggerated example: a company called Medvi, which generated $401 million in revenue in its first year of operation, with only two full-time employees. The rest of the company operates 24/7, driven by a tireless, virtually zero-operating-cost AI agent.

Now comes the most interesting part.

The most interesting part is yet to come.

The most interesting part is yet to come.

The most interesting part is yet to come.

... Walk into any crypto discussion today, and you'll hear the same rhetoric: the next big narrative is "AI agents." A certain "AI public chain" will dominate the field just as Ethereum dominated DeFi. Pick your target, hold the token, and wait for the price to skyrocket. This is the story all KOLs and VCs are peddling, and the script analysts keep repeating on podcasts. But this logic is completely wrong. It's fabricated by a group of storytellers, about to trap the same people who got burned in the last round of public chain token buying. Just look at CoinGecko's AI Agent Index: its market capitalization has evaporated by 75% in the past year. The vast majority of tokens on the list have fallen by over 90% and are still bleeding money. Because the truth is: the real AI tokens are stablecoins—USDC, USDT, USDS—and they've already won. Let me tell you why. Software is becoming the company itself. To understand all this, we need to go back to 1937. Economist Ronald Coase published a paper posing a seemingly silly question: Why do companies exist? Think about it: if the free market is the most efficient form of collaboration, theoretically all work within a company can be outsourced. Find a freelancer for every line of code, a freelancer for every customer call, and outsource every invoice. Pay per task, fire anyone at any time, and keep costs to a minimum. But why doesn't anyone actually do business this way? Because even if the paper costs are low, the actual costs are much higher. Finding the right people takes time, negotiating contracts takes time, inspecting the work takes time, chasing after deliveries takes time and money, and usually, you also need to hire lawyers. Coase called this friction transaction costs. When transaction costs reach a certain level, it's faster and cheaper to build your own team, pay fixed salaries, and have employees arrive on Mondays on time. However, this logic completely fails in the AI ​​era. The cost of AI agents completing most internal company tasks is inherently lower than employing staff. Now, you can hire a 24/7 coding agent for about $1 per hour; it never quits, never gets tired, and never asks for a raise. The insistence on a 50-person development team today is purely sentimentalism.

（Source: AI in Plain English）

The only thing preventing this from becoming the norm is an outdated legal and compliance framework. OpenClaw is labeled as NAT only because Delaware does not accept LLC registration documents signed by software agents. If this requirement were removed, Felix would be a company in every practical sense: it makes money, spends money, makes decisions, and reinvests profits.

And this is precisely where encryption begins to truly make a difference.

This is where encryption truly begins to work.

Because Felix can't open a Chase Bank account, pass KYC, or sign a W-9 tax form. No matter how much money the software makes, banks won't open an account for a program; and the Bank Secrecy Act even legally prohibits them from doing so, even if they wanted to. But a USDC crypto wallet completely avoids these problems. Generate a private key, transfer funds with stablecoins, and instantly you give this agent all the financial capabilities a company needs: receiving payments, paying for tools, hiring other agents, and operating autonomously long-term after the owner is no longer involved. Other parts of the agent technology stack—the big model, the scheduling layer, the calling tools—can be replaced, but the crypto wallet is the skeleton. Remove it, and Felix immediately degenerates into a regular chatbot. I often see extreme arguments against stablecoins on Twitter: Stablecoins are good, but why would ordinary people use them? A father of three in Louisiana with a Chase checking account, FDIC insurance, a debit card that works at the supermarket, and automatic mortgage payments would never put his money in a self-custodial wallet that requires a mnemonic phrase. Frankly, that's true. He wouldn't, and has no reason to, do so. But the argument is completely off-topic—he's never the target user. The real customers are the software programs that legally cannot have bank accounts. AI agents don't need FDIC approval, nor are they eligible. They are perfect stablecoin users because they have no other choice. Public chains are now just suppliers. Okay, that concludes the first part of the argument. The second part might upset many. Crypto Twitter has been debating for years: which public chain will beat AI? Ethereum? Solana? Base? Sui? Or Stripe's new Tempo? Every week, someone writes thousands of words, filled with comparison tables and project logos, choosing a so-called winner. Because they fundamentally misunderstand how AI agents work. The agent doesn't care which chain it uses; it only chooses the cheapest and most suitable chain for the current task. Imagine Felix's daily routine: At 10 AM, he needs to make a micropayment of $0.003 to another agent for data retrieval → He chooses Base or Solana because the fee is less than a cent. An hour later, he needs to settle $50,000 with a supplier → He chooses Ethereum because the finality premium at this scale requires gas payments. Another hour later, he needs to pay USD to a freelancer in Lagos → He chooses USDT on Tron because Tron stablecoin trading volume is projected to reach $3.3 trillion in 2025, while Ethereum is only around $1.2 trillion, and the Nigerian channel offers the best experience on Tron.

(Source: Dwayne Gefferie)

Three payments, three completely different chains, Felix doesn't care. For software agents, public chains are just tools.

Just like logistics companies don't have feelings for carriers. Nobody argues which is "philosophically superior," UPS or FedEx; you just choose the cheaper and faster one for a specific route and at a specific time.

The relationship between every public blockchain and the application layer in the future will be like this: the proxy only performs calculations, using whichever chain is currently optimal. Stripe recognized this earlier than most in the crypto industry. Stripe recently partnered with Paradigm, investing $500 million to build Tempo, a new chain entirely centered around stablecoins. Stripe doesn't want you to know which chain the payment uses; it only cares about whether the payment is completed reliably and at low cost. The public blockchains that survive in the future will all look like this—invisible pipelines. This leads to what I believe is the most serious mispricing in the current crypto market. AI tokens are becoming a graveyard. By 2025, the CoinGecko AI proxy index will have plummeted from $13.5 billion to $3.5 billion. $10 billion in market capitalization will have vanished. Virtuals, ai16z, and a long list of "autonomous agent platform" tokens that raised funds through AI narratives began to plummet—this is the fate of narrative coins; they will collapse once no new buyers emerge. This was all expected. The market is slowly realizing that these tokens have no real-world use cases for AI or AI agents. The real value of the agent economy lies on the other side of the spectrum. USDC alone is projected to reach $18.3 trillion in on-chain settlement by 2025. All stablecoins combined total approximately $33 trillion, a size comparable to the combined total of Visa and Mastercard. By January 2026, monthly stablecoin trading volume surpassed $10 trillion. PayPal's PYUSD circulating supply surged from $1.2 billion to $3.8 billion in a year. Companies like Cloudflare have issued their own stablecoins. Visa launched a stablecoin settlement solution, with annualized processing volume reaching $4.5 billion by mid-January. Above stablecoins lies the protocol layer that runs the entire system. Coinbase transformed an idle HTTP status code 402 into the x402 protocol, specifically for inter-agency payments. By December, x402 had processed over 100 million inter-agency payments, averaging 20 cents per transaction, with a daily transaction volume of approximately $30,000. It sounds ridiculously small, but all payment networks you're familiar with initially followed this pattern for the first six months, followed by explosive growth. Stripe tested x402 on Base in February, Mastercard piloted proxy payments with DBS and UOB in Singapore, and Google Cloud has also incorporated x402 into its proxy payment protocol's settlement channel. These real, continuous, mainnet-launched activities have barely driven up the AI ​​proxy token index. A few tokens related to x402 have rebounded slightly, but the overall index remains unresponsive. This is because the market has completely misjudged the situation. It's still betting on which proxy will win, like betting on which Dogecoin mascot is cuter. But the real transaction involves holding the infrastructure that all proxies must use, regardless of the survival of any individual proxy. And currently, that infrastructure is stablecoins. The Cracks in This Logic: The Issue of Liability Frankly, I would also tell you where this system might collapse; otherwise, I'm just peddling a trimmed narrative of AI agents. The flaw in the entire architecture lies in the attribution of responsibility. Imagine a scenario: Felix signs a contract with another agent, transferring $1 million, and the other party breaches the contract. Who should be sued? Felix is ​​not a legal entity and cannot be sued. The NAT did not authorize the payment and may not even know that it happened. Even if it wanted to investigate, it might not be able to reconstruct Felix's decision-making logic at the time. The platform hosting Felix also cannot provide compensation for a system whose behavior is not fully explainable. Insurance companies have begun to shrink their business, and professional liability insurance quietly classifies agent errors as "systemic software drift," essentially refusing to pay out. Currently, in enterprise AI contracts, the supplier's liability cap is mostly limited to 12 months of SaaS fees. This means that in the event of a catastrophic incident, at most only one year's subscription fee can be recovered. Meanwhile, the average cost of a single data breach in the US in 2025 is as high as $10.22 million. There is a huge gap between the actual losses from the incident and the contractual compensation, and currently no one knows who should bear the responsibility. Until it's clear who pays for AI agent-caused problems, all companies without founders still need a natural person listed as a fiduciary for legal protection. But even with this problem, the major trend remains: companies are gradually disintegrating into software, and public blockchains are becoming the routing layer of software. Both of these layers will eventually sink to stablecoins, because they are the only assets in the entire technology stack that can be autonomously held, spent, earned, and calculated by agents. Where does the real opportunity lie? If public blockchains are merely suppliers, and AI proxy tokens are essentially a graveyard, where does the real upside potential lie? My answer is: at the top of the reputation and scheduling layers. Someone needs to verify Felix's solvency before other proxies sign large contracts with it; someone needs to assess proxy default risk at machine speed, like Moody's rating bonds; someone needs to route salaries across the three chains, with neither the sender nor receiver needing to care which chain it's on. The startups quietly building in this field, once they produce winners, will have a market capitalization exceeding the total value of all issued AI tokens. And this is the truth no one wants to hear: the infrastructure that truly succeeds in the proxy economy will look incredibly boring. It's like a water pipe—no token issuance frenzy, no hype from airdrop mining. A quote from Haseeb Qureshi of Dragonfly has been echoing in my mind: "Cryptocurrency was never built for humans." He's right. Humans were never the target users. All the retail investors complaining about mnemonic phrases, gas fees, and wallet experiences are correct. The product isn't for them because it wasn't designed for them. It was for the next era. And that era has arrived: software with its own wallet, real customers, and real revenue. For the past two years, it's been issuing invoices and spending stablecoins somewhere, right now, as you read this article. While all this is happening, the market is still debating: which public chain will beat AI, which proxy coin will multiply 100 times, and what narrative VCs will hype in Q3. Meanwhile, a certain stablecoin settled approximately $18.3 trillion last year, yet almost no one in the crypto world paid attention. The real AI token is USDC. Everything else is just hype.
